Q1: I tried to keep things relatively simple with my study routine. I'd usually set aside a few hours on weekends and maybe one or two evenings during the week, aiming for around ten hours total. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found I needed to actually do things with the material rather than just read it, so I'd often work through practice problems or try to apply concepts hands-on. It wasn't the most structured approach, but it worked well enough for me to get by.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. When deadlines approached, I'd put in more concentrated effort, but during quieter weeks I might slack off a bit. I tried to maintain some consistency, but honestly life gets in the way sometimes, and I'd adjust accordingly. The flexibility helped me manage without getting too stressed about sticking to a rigid schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or assignments posted. I'd log in maybe once a day or so, just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that would prompt me to check, or I'd remember I needed to look at a deadline. The fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator for me to keep checking.
Q4: It did become somewhat routine after the first few weeks. I'd usually check the LMS in the morning with my coffee, just a quick scan to see what was new. It wasn't like a deep engagement every time, more like checking my email. The habit formed naturally because I was scared of falling behind or missing important information about assignments.
Q5: The video content was actually really helpful for me because I could pause and practice along with what was being demonstrated. I watched quite a bit of video content because it felt more engaging than just reading. The discussion forums were useful too when I was confused about something—seeing how other students interpreted things helped me understand concepts better. Anything that let me actively engage rather than passively read felt more valuable.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't always complete all the readings, especially the longer supplementary ones. If something felt like it was just extra background information rather than directly relevant to assignments, I'd often skim it or skip it entirely. Some of the optional practice quizzes I ignored because I figured I'd learn more from working on actual assignments. Time is limited, so I had to prioritize what seemed most directly useful.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to what I'd be assessed on or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. If I could apply it somehow—like practice problems or hands-on exercises—that made it feel more valuable. Watching a video where someone demonstrated a process step by step felt worthwhile because I could follow along. Abstract readings without clear application felt less valuable to me.
Q8: Required activities obviously got priority because they directly affected my grade. Optional activities felt more like a luxury—nice to have if I had time, but often pushed aside when things got busy. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping optional stuff, but with limited time and energy, I had to make choices. The required work always came first, and optional things only happened if I was genuinely curious or had extra time.
Q9: Definitely yes. I'd often download PDFs and work through them on my tablet away from the computer. I also used physical notebooks a lot—writing things out by hand helps me learn better than just clicking through screens. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, take notes, and then study from my notes later without going back to the LMS. A lot of my actual learning happened with paper and pen in front of me.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems in my notebook or reviewing my handwritten notes, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also sometimes discuss concepts with a classmate over coffee, working through ideas together verbally. My LMS activity might look pretty minimal during those times, but I was definitely engaged with the material. The system just couldn't see what I was doing offline.
Q11: Yes, that happened regularly. I'd download lecture slides or readings, maybe watch a video once to get the gist, and then work with those materials offline for hours. My apartment has decent internet, but I found I focused better when I wasn't connected and couldn't get distracted by notifications. So a single download might represent several hours of subsequent study that wouldn't show up in any logs.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when I felt scared about whether I was understanding things correctly or when multiple deadlines converged. The CBT session I had actually helped me work through some of that anxiety. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd sometimes freeze up and avoid the LMS for a day or two, which probably wasn't helpful but felt necessary in the moment. Those periods of avoidance were usually followed by catch-up sessions.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor for me. After a long day, I just didn't have the mental energy to engage deeply with course material, so I'd do minimal check-ins rather than actual studying. Competing responsibilities like work and personal stuff definitely ate into my study time some weeks. When I was tired or stressed, I'd still log in but mostly just to check deadlines rather than to actually learn anything.
Q14: Oh, absolutely. There were times I'd be scrolling through readings or watching videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd reach the end of something and realize I hadn't absorbed any of it. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'using' the LMS. Being physically present with the material isn't the same as actually engaging with it mentally.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially when I was scared about not understanding the material well enough. Sometimes avoiding an assignment felt easier than confronting how confused I was. I'd tell myself I needed more preparation time, but really I was just anxious about failing. The CBT work helped me recognize this pattern, but I still struggled with it throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—watching videos I'd put off, checking discussion posts for hints, submitting work. Without deadlines, I probably would have been even less engaged with the platform. They created urgency that cut through my tendency to avoid things I was scared about.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though sometimes not in the way instructors probably intended. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me scared to try again. I'd wait longer before checking my grades because I was anxious about what I'd find. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me know what to focus on next.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me pace myself, even if I didn't always follow the suggested timeline perfectly. The structure made it clear what was expected, which reduced some of my anxiety about not knowing what to do. Without that structure, I probably would have been even more scattered in my approach.
Q19: Having videos alongside readings made engagement easier because I could choose what format worked for me in the moment. The discussion forums were helpful when I was confused. What made things harder was when instructions were vague—that triggered my anxiety about doing things wrong. Also, having too many small tasks to track could feel overwhelming and made me want to disengage.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, looking for clarity, but still feel too uncertain to submit anything. It could also be anxiety-related—being active because you're scared of missing something, but too paralyzed to actually complete assignments. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That actually describes me to some extent. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in the LMS—working through problems by hand, studying from downloaded materials, discussing with classmates. Someone could look at my login frequency and think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in the work in ways the system couldn't track. Performance isn't always correlated with visible online activity.
Q22: A course log can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, and whether you submit things on time. It can track the quantity of your interactions. But it completely misses the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just going through the motions. It can't see offline study, emotional state, or whether you're genuinely learning versus just checking boxes.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and trying to connect it to things I already know or can apply. It's not just being present or clicking through content—it's having my brain actively working on understanding something. For me as a kinesthetic learner, engagement often means doing something with the material, not just consuming it. It's about genuine mental investment, not just time spent.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent studying with my notebook and downloaded materials away from the computer. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I felt about the course or how that affected my engagement patterns. It wouldn't show the conversations I had with classmates or the moments when something finally clicked for me. My emotional journey through the course—the fear, the small victories, the coping strategies from CBT—none of that appears in any log.
